notes: Extracted in single pass (50 paragraphs, dense with Scripture quotations and Josephus-derived siege narrative). Footnote markers (e.g. '(1)') stripped from quote fields since they are apparatus, not sentence text. Many paragraphs contain multiple biblical quotations attributed by the text to a prophet/psalmist/Jesus/Paul; each is logged as a separate attributed_quote claim because each is independently checkable for source and accuracy. Block quotation in paragraphs 41-43 is unattributed by name in the running text (footnote 46) but presented as a historian's account of the temple's burning; logged as event claims.

claims.quote: “If thou hadst known, even thou, at least in this thy day, the things which belong unto thy peace! but now they are hid from thine eyes. For the days shall come upon thee, that thine enemies shall cast a trench about thee, and compass thee round, and keep thee in on every side, and shall lay thee even with the ground, and thy children within thee; and they shall not leave in thee one stone upon another; because thou knewest not the time of thy visitation.”
claims.claim_text: According to the Gospel of Luke, Jesus prophesied over Jerusalem that enemies would surround it, hem it in on every side, level it and its children (inhabitants) to the ground, and leave not one stone upon another, because it did not recognize the time of its visitation.

claims.quote: From the crest of Olivet, Jesus looked upon Jerusalem. Fair and peaceful was the scene spread out before Him. It was the season of the Passover, and from all lands the children of Jacob had gathered there to celebrate the great national festival.
claims.claim_text: Jesus looked upon Jerusalem from the Mount of Olives during the season of Passover, when Jews from many lands had gathered in the city to celebrate the festival.

claims.quote: The daughter of Zion seemed in her pride to say, “I sit a queen, and shall see no sorrow;”
claims.claim_text: The text attributes to personified Jerusalem ('the daughter of Zion') the boast 'I sit a queen, and shall see no sorrow,' language paralleling scriptural phrases used elsewhere (e.g., Revelation 18:7, Isaiah 47:8).
claims.extractor_note: Applied typologically to Jerusalem rather than a direct scriptural statement about Jerusalem; worth checking source attribution.

claims.quote: as when, ages before, the royal minstrel sung, “Beautiful for situation, the joy of the whole earth, is Mount Zion, ... the city of the great King.”
claims.claim_text: The text attributes to 'the royal minstrel' (the psalmist, traditionally David) the words 'Beautiful for situation, the joy of the whole earth, is Mount Zion ... the city of the great King' (cf. Psalm 48:2).

claims.quote: “The perfection of beauty” it stood, the pride of the Jewish nation.
claims.claim_text: The text applies to the Jerusalem temple the biblical phrase 'the perfection of beauty,' language used in Lamentations 2:15 of Jerusalem.

claims.quote: “When He was come near, He beheld the city, and wept over it.”
claims.claim_text: According to the Gospel account, when Jesus approached Jerusalem he saw the city and wept over it.

claims.quote: Amid the universal rejoicing of the triumphal entry, while palm branches waved, while glad hosannas awoke the echoes of the hills, and thousands of voices declared Him king, the world’s Redeemer was overwhelmed with a sudden and mysterious sorrow.
claims.claim_text: During Jesus' triumphal entry into Jerusalem, crowds waved palm branches and thousands of voices declared him king, while Jesus himself was overcome with sudden sorrow.

claims.quote: Before Him lay Gethsemane, the scene of His approaching agony.
claims.claim_text: Gethsemane, the site of Jesus' coming agony, was visible from the vantage point on the Mount of Olives where he wept over Jerusalem.

claims.quote: The sheep gate also was in sight, through which for centuries the victims for sacrifice had been led, and which was to open for Him when He should be “brought as a lamb to the slaughter.”
claims.claim_text: The Sheep Gate of Jerusalem, through which sacrificial animals had been led for centuries, was visible from the same vantage point.

claims.quote: “brought as a lamb to the slaughter.”
claims.claim_text: The text applies to Jesus' coming crucifixion the prophetic phrase 'brought as a lamb to the slaughter' (Isaiah 53:7).

claims.quote: Not far distant was Calvary, the place of crucifixion.
claims.claim_text: Calvary, the site of the crucifixion, lay not far from the vantage point on the Mount of Olives.

claims.quote: He wept for the doomed thousands of Jerusalem—because of the blindness and impenitence of those whom He came to bless and to save.
claims.claim_text: Jesus wept specifically over the future doom of Jerusalem's inhabitants because of their spiritual blindness and impenitence.

claims.quote: There was Mount Moriah, where the son of promise, an unresisting victim, had been bound to the altar,—emblem of the offering of the Son of God.
claims.claim_text: Mount Moriah was the site where Isaac, 'the son of promise,' was bound to the altar as a sacrifice (Genesis 22).

claims.quote: There, the flames of the sacrifice ascending to heaven from the threshing-floor of Ornan had turned aside the sword of the destroying angel—fitting symbol of the Saviour’s sacrifice and mediation for guilty men.
claims.claim_text: At the threshing floor of Ornan, the ascending flames of sacrifice turned aside the sword of the destroying angel (referencing 1 Chronicles 21 / 2 Samuel 24).

claims.quote: The Lord had “chosen Zion,” He had “desired it for His habitation.”
claims.claim_text: The text attributes to the Lord, via Scripture, the statements that he 'chose Zion' and 'desired it for His habitation' (cf. Psalm 132:13-14).

claims.quote: Had Israel as a nation preserved her allegiance to Heaven, Jerusalem would have stood forever, the elect of God.
claims.claim_text: Had Israel preserved its allegiance to Heaven, Jerusalem would have stood forever as God's elect city.

claims.quote: Although Israel had “mocked the messengers of God, and despised His words, and misused His prophets,”
claims.claim_text: The text attributes to Scripture (2 Chronicles 36:16) the statement that Israel mocked God's messengers, despised his words, and misused his prophets.

claims.quote: He had still manifested Himself to them, as “the Lord God, merciful and gracious, long-suffering, and abundant in goodness and truth;”
claims.claim_text: The text attributes to Scripture (Exodus 34:6) the description of God as 'the Lord God, merciful and gracious, long-suffering, and abundant in goodness and truth.'

claims.quote: God had “sent to them by His messengers, rising up betimes, and sending; because He had compassion on His people, and on His dwelling-place.”
claims.claim_text: The text attributes to Scripture (2 Chronicles 36:15) the statement that God repeatedly sent messengers to his people out of compassion.

claims.quote: It was Christ that had brought Israel as a goodly vine out of Egypt.(13) His own hand had cast out the heathen before it. He had planted it “in a very fruitful hill.”
claims.claim_text: The text asserts that Christ brought Israel out of Egypt as a 'goodly vine' and planted it 'in a very fruitful hill,' referencing Psalm 80:8 and Isaiah 5:1.

claims.quote: “What could have been done more to My vineyard,” He exclaims, “that I have not done in it?”
claims.claim_text: The text attributes to God, via Isaiah 5:4, the rhetorical question 'What could have been done more to My vineyard, that I have not done in it?'

claims.quote: Though when He “looked that it should bring forth grapes, it brought forth wild grapes,”
claims.claim_text: The text attributes to Isaiah 5:2/4 the statement that when God looked for the vineyard (Israel) to bring forth grapes, it brought forth wild grapes instead.

claims.quote: For three years the Lord of light and glory had gone in and out among His people.
claims.claim_text: Jesus' public ministry among his people lasted three years.

claims.quote: He “went about doing good, and healing all that were oppressed of the devil,”
claims.claim_text: The text attributes to Scripture (Acts 10:38) the statement that Jesus 'went about doing good, and healing all that were oppressed of the devil.'

claims.quote: To all classes alike was addressed the gracious call, “Come unto Me, all ye that labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest.”
claims.claim_text: The text attributes to Jesus, via Matthew 11:28, the invitation 'Come unto Me, all ye that labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest.'

claims.quote: But Israel had turned from her best Friend and only Helper. The pleadings of His love had been despised, His counsels spurned, His warnings ridiculed.
claims.claim_text: Israel is characterized as having turned from Christ, despising his pleadings, spurning his counsels, and ridiculing his warnings.

claims.quote: When Christ should hang upon the cross of Calvary, Israel’s day as a nation favored and blessed of God would be ended.
claims.claim_text: The crucifixion of Christ marked the end of Israel's status as a nation specially favored and blessed by God.

claims.quote: Jeremiah wished that his eyes were a fountain of tears, that he might weep day and night for the slain of the daughter of his people, for the Lord’s flock that was carried away captive.
claims.claim_text: The prophet Jeremiah wished his eyes were a fountain of tears so he could weep for the slain of his people and for the Lord's flock carried into captivity (referencing Jeremiah 9:1, 13:17).

claims.quote: From the ridge of Olivet, the very spot afterward occupied by Titus and his army, He looked across the valley upon the sacred courts and porticoes,
claims.claim_text: The Roman general Titus and his army later occupied the ridge of the Mount of Olives, the same spot from which Jesus had earlier looked upon Jerusalem, during the siege of the city.

claims.quote: ‘O Jerusalem, Jerusalem, thou that killest the prophets, and stonest them which are sent unto thee, how often would I have gathered thy children together, even as a hen gathereth her chickens under her wings, and ye would not!’
claims.claim_text: The text attributes to Jesus, via Matthew 23:37, the lament 'O Jerusalem, Jerusalem, thou that killest the prophets, and stonest them which are sent unto thee, how often would I have gathered thy children together, even as a hen gathereth her chickens under her wings, and ye would not!'

claims.quote: ‘Ye will not come to Me, that ye might have life.’
claims.claim_text: The text attributes to Jesus, via John 5:40, the statement 'Ye will not come to Me, that ye might have life.'

claims.quote: Christ saw in Jerusalem a symbol of the world hardened in unbelief and rebellion, and hastening on to meet the retributive judgments of God.
claims.claim_text: Christ regarded Jerusalem as a symbol of a world hardened in unbelief and rebellion, hastening toward God's retributive judgments.

claims.quote: The great sin of the Jews was their rejection of Christ; the great sin of the Christian world would be their rejection of the law of God, the foundation of His government in heaven and earth.
claims.claim_text: The great sin of the Jews was their rejection of Christ, paralleled by the future great sin of the Christian world being rejection of the law of God.

claims.quote: Two days before the Passover, when Christ had for the last time departed from the temple, after denouncing the hypocrisy of the Jewish rulers, He again went out with His disciples to the Mount of Olives, and seated Himself with them upon the grassy slope overlooking the city.
claims.claim_text: Two days before Passover, after denouncing the Jewish rulers' hypocrisy and departing the temple for the last time, Jesus went with his disciples to the Mount of Olives and sat overlooking Jerusalem.

claims.quote: A thousand years before, the psalmist had magnified God’s favor to Israel in making her holy house His dwelling-place: “In Salem also is His tabernacle, and His dwelling-place in Zion.”
claims.claim_text: About a thousand years before the events of this chapter, the psalmist wrote of God's favor to Israel in making the temple his dwelling place, in the words 'In Salem also is His tabernacle, and His dwelling-place in Zion' (Psalm 76:2).

claims.quote: He “chose the tribe of Judah, the Mount Zion which He loved. And He built His sanctuary like high palaces.”
claims.claim_text: The text attributes to Scripture (Psalm 78:68-69) the statement that God 'chose the tribe of Judah, the Mount Zion which He loved,' and built his sanctuary 'like high palaces.'

claims.quote: The first temple had been erected during the most prosperous period of Israel’s history. Vast stores of treasure for this purpose had been collected by King David, and the plans for its construction were made by divine inspiration.(25) Solomon, the wisest of Israel’s monarchs, had completed the work.
claims.claim_text: The first Jerusalem temple was built during Israel's most prosperous historical period; King David collected vast treasure for its construction and its plans, and King Solomon completed the building.
claims.extractor_note: The claim that the plans were made 'by divine inspiration' is a theological assertion bundled with the historical claim about David/Solomon's roles; flagged together but the historical portion (David's collection of treasure, Solomon completing construction) is independently checkable.

claims.quote: This temple was the most magnificent building which the world ever saw.
claims.claim_text: Solomon's temple was the most magnificent building the world had ever seen.

claims.quote: Yet the Lord had declared by the prophet Haggai, concerning the second temple, “The glory of this latter house shall be greater than of the former.” “I will shake all nations, and the Desire of all nations shall come: and I will fill this house with glory, saith the Lord of hosts.”
claims.claim_text: The text attributes to the prophet Haggai (Haggai 2:7,9) the prophecy that the glory of the second temple would be greater than that of the first, and that God would 'shake all nations' and fill the house with glory.

claims.quote: After the destruction of the temple by Nebuchadnezzar, it was rebuilt about five hundred years before the birth of Christ, by a people who from a life-long captivity had returned to a wasted and almost deserted country.
claims.claim_text: After Nebuchadnezzar destroyed the first Jerusalem temple, it was rebuilt about 500 years before the birth of Christ by Jews returning from captivity.

claims.quote: There were then among them aged men who had seen the glory of Solomon’s temple, and who wept at the foundation of the new building, that it must be so inferior to the former.
claims.claim_text: When the second temple's foundation was laid, elderly men present who remembered Solomon's original temple wept because the new building appeared inferior (referencing Ezra 3:12).

claims.quote: “Who is left among you that saw this house in her first glory? and how do ye see it now? is it not in your eyes in comparison of it as nothing?”
claims.claim_text: The text attributes to the prophet Haggai (Haggai 2:3) the rhetorical question about those who remembered the first temple's glory viewing the second temple as inferior.

claims.quote: There was no manifestation of supernatural power to mark its dedication. No cloud of glory was seen to fill the newly erected sanctuary. No fire from heaven descended to consume the sacrifice upon its altar. The shekinah no longer abode between the cherubim in the most holy place; the ark, the mercy-seat, and the tables of the testimony were not to be found therein.
claims.claim_text: Unlike the first temple, the dedication of the second temple was not marked by a cloud of glory or fire from heaven, and the ark of the covenant, mercy seat, and tables of the testimony were absent from it.
claims.extractor_note: Combines a historically checkable claim (absence of the Ark from the Second Temple) with theological framing (absence of supernatural manifestations); kept together as the paragraph presents them as one description.

claims.quote: The second temple was not honored with the cloud of Jehovah’s glory, but with the living presence of One in whom dwelt the fulness of the Godhead bodily—who was God Himself manifest in the flesh.
claims.claim_text: The second temple's true glory, exceeding that of the first, was the incarnate presence of Christ rather than any visible manifestation.

claims.quote: Already were the Saviour’s words fulfilled, “Your house is left unto you desolate.”
claims.claim_text: The text attributes to Jesus, via Matthew 23:38, the pronouncement 'Your house is left unto you desolate.'

claims.quote: Wealth, labor, and architectural skill had for more than forty years been freely expended to enhance its splendors.
claims.claim_text: By the time of Jesus, the Herodian embellishment of the Jerusalem temple had involved more than forty years of continuous expenditure of wealth, labor, and architectural skill.

claims.quote: Herod the Great had lavished upon it both Roman wealth and Jewish treasure, and even the emperor of the world had enriched it with his gifts.
claims.claim_text: Herod the Great lavished both Roman and Jewish wealth upon the Jerusalem temple, and the Roman emperor also contributed gifts to it.

claims.quote: Massive blocks of white marble, of almost fabulous size, forwarded from Rome for this purpose, formed a part of its structure;
claims.claim_text: Massive blocks of white marble sent from Rome formed part of the structure of the Jerusalem temple.

claims.quote: to these the disciples had called the attention of their Master, saying, “See what manner of stones and what buildings are here!”
claims.claim_text: The text attributes to the disciples, via Mark 13:1, the exclamation to Jesus, 'See what manner of stones and what buildings are here!'

claims.quote: To these words, Jesus made the solemn and startling reply, “Verily I say unto you, There shall not be left here one stone upon another, that shall not be thrown down.”
claims.claim_text: The text attributes to Jesus, via Matthew 24:2, the reply 'Verily I say unto you, There shall not be left here one stone upon another, that shall not be thrown down.'

claims.quote: With the overthrow of Jerusalem the disciples associated the events of Christ’s personal coming in temporal glory to take the throne of universal empire, to punish the impenitent Jews, and to break from off the nation the Roman yoke.
claims.claim_text: Jesus' disciples believed that the overthrow of Jerusalem would coincide with Christ's coming in temporal glory to take a universal throne, punish impenitent Jews, and free the nation from Roman rule.

claims.quote: they asked, “When shall these things be? and what shall be the sign of Thy coming, and of the end of the world?”
claims.claim_text: The text attributes to the disciples, via Matthew 24:3, the question 'When shall these things be? and what shall be the sign of Thy coming, and of the end of the world?'

claims.quote: The prophecy which He uttered was twofold in its meaning: while foreshadowing the destruction of Jerusalem, it prefigured also the terrors of the last great day.
claims.claim_text: Christ's prophecy on the Mount of Olives had a twofold meaning, foreshadowing both the destruction of Jerusalem and the terrors of the last day.

claims.quote: the Saviour warned His followers: “When ye therefore shall see the abomination of desolation, spoken of by Daniel the prophet, stand in the holy place, (whoso readeth, let him understand:) then let them which be in Judea flee into the mountains.”
claims.claim_text: The text attributes to Jesus, via Matthew 24:15-16, the warning that when the 'abomination of desolation' spoken of by Daniel is seen standing in the holy place, those in Judea should flee to the mountains.

claims.quote: When the idolatrous standards of the Romans should be set up in the holy ground, which extended some furlongs outside the city walls, then the followers of Christ were to find safety in flight.
claims.claim_text: Jerusalem's 'holy ground' extended some furlongs beyond the city walls, and the sign for Christians to flee was the setting up of Roman military standards on that ground.

claims.quote: In the reign of Herod, Jerusalem had not only been greatly beautified, but by the erection of towers, walls, and fortresses, adding to the natural strength of its situation, it had been rendered apparently impregnable.
claims.claim_text: During Herod's reign, Jerusalem was greatly beautified and fortified with towers, walls, and fortresses, making it appear impregnable.

claims.quote: But Christ had said, “Heaven and earth shall pass away, but My words shall not pass away.”
claims.claim_text: The text attributes to Christ, via Matthew 24:35, the statement 'Heaven and earth shall pass away, but My words shall not pass away.'

claims.quote: The Lord had declared by the prophet Micah: “Hear this, I pray you, ye heads of the house of Jacob, and princes of the house of Israel, that abhor judgment, and pervert all equity. They build up Zion with blood, and Jerusalem with iniquity. The heads thereof judge for reward, and the priests thereof teach for hire, and the prophets thereof divine for money: yet will they lean upon the Lord, and say, Is not the Lord among us? none evil can come upon us.”
claims.claim_text: The text attributes to the prophet Micah (Micah 3:9-11) a denunciation of Israel's and Jerusalem's leaders, priests, and prophets for corruption while presuming God's protection.

claims.quote: “If we let Him thus alone,” said the Jewish leaders, “all men will believe on Him: and the Romans shall come and take away both our place and nation.”
claims.claim_text: The text attributes to the Jewish leaders, via John 11:48, the statement 'If we let Him thus alone, all men will believe on Him: and the Romans shall come and take away both our place and nation.'

claims.quote: Thus they reasoned, and they concurred in the decision of their high priest, that it would be better for one man to die than for the whole nation to perish.
claims.claim_text: The Jewish leaders concurred with their high priest's decision that it would be better for Jesus to die than for the whole nation to perish (referencing Caiaphas in John 11:49-50).

claims.quote: “Therefore,” continued the prophet, “shall Zion for your sake be plowed as a field, and Jerusalem shall become heaps, and the mountain of the house as the high places of the forest.”
claims.claim_text: The text attributes to the prophet Micah (Micah 3:12) the prediction that Zion would be plowed as a field, Jerusalem would become heaps, and the temple mount would become like forest high places.

claims.quote: For nearly forty years after the doom of Jerusalem had been pronounced by Christ Himself, the Lord delayed His judgments upon the city and the nation.
claims.claim_text: Approximately forty years passed between Christ's pronouncement of Jerusalem's doom and the city's eventual destruction.

claims.quote: The command had gone forth, “Cut it down; why cumbereth it the ground?”
claims.claim_text: The text applies to the Jewish nation the parabolic command from Luke 13:7, 'Cut it down; why cumbereth it the ground?'

claims.quote: By their actions they had long been saying, “Cause the Holy One of Israel to cease from before us.”
claims.claim_text: The text applies to the Jews, via Isaiah 30:11, the sentiment 'Cause the Holy One of Israel to cease from before us.'

claims.quote: Then God withdrew His protection from them, and removed His restraining power from Satan and his angels, and the nation was left to the control of the leader she had chosen.
claims.claim_text: God withdrew his protection from the Jewish nation and removed his restraining power over Satan, leaving the nation under Satan's control.

claims.quote: In the family and in the nation, among the highest and the lowest classes alike, there was suspicion, envy, hatred, strife, rebellion, murder. There was no safety anywhere. Friends and kindred betrayed one another. Parents slew their children and children their parents.
claims.claim_text: In the period before Jerusalem's fall, Jewish society at all levels was marked by suspicion, betrayal, and violence, including instances of parents and children killing one another.

claims.quote: The leaders of the opposing factions at times united to plunder and torture their wretched victims, and again they fell upon each other’s forces, and slaughtered without mercy.
claims.claim_text: Leaders of rival Jewish factions within besieged Jerusalem sometimes united to plunder and torture victims, and at other times fought and slaughtered one another.

claims.quote: The worshipers were stricken down before the altar, and the sanctuary was polluted with the bodies of the slain.
claims.claim_text: During the internal Jewish factional violence, worshipers were killed before the temple altar and the sanctuary was polluted with corpses.

claims.quote: To establish their power more firmly, they bribed false prophets to proclaim, even while Roman legions were besieging the temple, that the people were to wait for deliverance from God.
claims.claim_text: Jewish faction leaders bribed false prophets to proclaim, even during the Roman siege of the temple, that the people should wait for divine deliverance.

claims.quote: All the predictions given by Christ concerning the destruction of Jerusalem were fulfilled to the letter.
claims.claim_text: All of Christ's predictions concerning the destruction of Jerusalem were fulfilled exactly.

claims.quote: The Jews experienced the truth of His words of warning, “With what measure ye mete, it shall be measured to you again.”
claims.claim_text: The text attributes to Jesus, via Matthew 7:2, the warning 'With what measure ye mete, it shall be measured to you again,' applied to the Jews' experience.

claims.quote: In the midst of the night an unnatural light shone over the temple and the altar.
claims.claim_text: An unnatural light was reported shining over the Jerusalem temple and altar at night before the city's destruction.

claims.quote: Upon the clouds at sunset were pictured chariots and men of war gathering for battle.
claims.claim_text: Images of chariots and armed men gathering for battle were reportedly seen in the clouds at sunset before Jerusalem's destruction.

claims.quote: The priests ministering by night in the sanctuary were terrified by mysterious sounds; the earth trembled, and a multitude of voices were heard crying, “Let us depart hence.”
claims.claim_text: Priests ministering at night in the Jerusalem temple reported being terrified by mysterious sounds, earth tremors, and voices crying 'Let us depart hence.'

claims.quote: The great eastern gate, which was so heavy that it could hardly be shut by a score of men, and which was secured by immense bars of iron fastened deep in the pavement of solid stone, opened at midnight, without visible agency.
claims.claim_text: The great eastern gate of the Jerusalem temple, normally requiring about twenty men to close and secured by iron bars set in stone, reportedly opened at midnight without visible cause.

claims.quote: For seven years a man continued to go up and down the streets of Jerusalem, declaring the woes that were to come upon the city.
claims.claim_text: A man walked the streets of Jerusalem for seven years proclaiming impending woes upon the city before its destruction.
claims.extractor_note: Corresponds to the figure of Jesus son of Ananias described by Josephus, Wars of the Jews 6.5.3.

claims.quote: By day and by night he chanted the wild dirge, “A voice from the east! a voice from the west! a voice from the four winds! a voice against Jerusalem and against the temple! a voice against the bridegrooms and the brides! a voice against the whole people!”
claims.claim_text: The man is quoted as chanting day and night the dirge 'A voice from the east! a voice from the west! a voice from the four winds! a voice against Jerusalem and against the temple! a voice against the bridegrooms and the brides! a voice against the whole people!'

claims.quote: This strange being was imprisoned and scourged, but no complaint escaped his lips. To insult and abuse he answered only, “Woe, woe to Jerusalem!” “woe, woe to the inhabitants thereof!” His warning cry ceased not until he was slain in the siege he had foretold.
claims.claim_text: The man proclaiming woes against Jerusalem was imprisoned and scourged, responded only with 'Woe, woe to Jerusalem,' and was killed during the siege he had foretold.

claims.quote: Not one Christian perished in the destruction of Jerusalem.
claims.claim_text: No Christian died in the destruction of Jerusalem in AD 70.

claims.quote: “When ye shall see Jerusalem compassed with armies,” said Jesus, “then know that the desolation thereof is nigh. Then let them which are in Judea flee to the mountains; and let them which are in the midst of it depart out.”
claims.claim_text: The text attributes to Jesus, via Luke 21:20-21, the instruction that when Jerusalem is seen surrounded by armies, those in Judea and the city should flee to the mountains.

claims.quote: After the Romans under Cestius had surrounded the city, they unexpectedly abandoned the siege when everything seemed favorable for an immediate attack.
claims.claim_text: The Roman forces under Cestius Gallus surrounded Jerusalem and then unexpectedly withdrew when conditions favored an immediate attack.

claims.quote: Upon the retreat of Cestius, the Jews, sallying from Jerusalem, pursued after his retiring army; and while both forces were thus fully engaged, the Christians had an opportunity to leave the city.
claims.claim_text: When Cestius retreated, Jewish forces pursued his army from Jerusalem, and the resulting engagement gave Christians in the city an opportunity to flee.

claims.quote: At the time of the siege, the Jews were assembled at Jerusalem to keep the Feast of Tabernacles, and thus the Christians throughout the land were able to make their escape unmolested.
claims.claim_text: The Jews were assembled in Jerusalem for the Feast of Tabernacles at the time of Cestius's siege, and this allowed Christians throughout the land to escape unmolested.

claims.quote: Without delay they fled to a place of safety,—the city of Pella, in the land of Perea, beyond Jordan.
claims.claim_text: Christians fleeing Jerusalem went to the city of Pella in the region of Perea, beyond the Jordan River.

claims.quote: The Jewish forces, pursuing after Cestius and his army, fell upon their rear with such fierceness as to threaten them with total destruction. It was with great difficulty that the Romans succeeded in making their retreat.
claims.claim_text: Jewish forces pursuing Cestius's retreating army attacked its rear so fiercely that the Romans nearly suffered total destruction and retreated only with great difficulty.

claims.quote: The Jews escaped almost without loss, and with their spoils returned in triumph to Jerusalem.
claims.claim_text: The Jewish forces suffered almost no losses in the engagement with Cestius's retreating army and returned to Jerusalem in triumph with spoils.

claims.quote: Yet this apparent success brought them only evil. It inspired them with that spirit of stubborn resistance to the Romans which speedily brought unutterable woe upon the doomed city.
claims.claim_text: The Jewish victory over Cestius's retreating forces inspired a spirit of stubborn resistance to Rome that hastened Jerusalem's eventual destruction.

claims.quote: Terrible were the calamities that fell upon Jerusalem when the siege was resumed by Titus. The city was invested at the time of the Passover, when millions of Jews were assembled within its walls.
claims.claim_text: Titus resumed the siege of Jerusalem at the time of Passover, when millions of Jews were gathered within the city walls.

claims.quote: Their stores of provision, which if carefully preserved would have supplied the inhabitants for years, had previously been destroyed through the jealousy and revenge of the contending factions, and now all the horrors of starvation were experienced.
claims.claim_text: Jerusalem's food stores, which could have supplied the population for years, were destroyed before the siege by rival Jewish factions out of jealousy and revenge, leading to starvation.

claims.quote: A measure of wheat was sold for a talent.
claims.claim_text: During the siege of Jerusalem, a measure of wheat was sold for a talent (an extremely high price reflecting severe famine).

claims.quote: So fierce were the pangs of hunger that men would gnaw the leather of their belts and sandals and the covering of their shields.
claims.claim_text: During the famine of the siege of Jerusalem, people were so hungry that they gnawed on the leather of belts, sandals, and shield coverings.

claims.quote: Great numbers of the people would steal out at night to gather wild plants growing outside the city walls, though many were seized and put to death with cruel torture, and often those who returned in safety were robbed of what they had gleaned at so great peril.
claims.claim_text: During the siege, many Jerusalem residents snuck out at night to gather wild plants outside the walls; many were caught and killed with torture, and survivors were often robbed of their gleanings.

claims.quote: The most inhuman tortures were inflicted by those in power, to force from the want-stricken people the last scanty supplies which they might have concealed.
claims.claim_text: Those in power within besieged Jerusalem inflicted severe torture on starving residents to extract any concealed food supplies.

claims.quote: Thousands perished from famine and pestilence.
claims.claim_text: Thousands of people died from famine and disease during the siege of Jerusalem.

claims.quote: Children would be seen snatching the food from the mouths of their aged parents.
claims.claim_text: During the famine of the siege, children were observed snatching food from their aged parents' mouths.

claims.quote: The question of the prophet, “Can a woman forget her sucking child?”(42) received the answer within the walls of that doomed city, “The hands of the pitiful women have sodden their own children: they were their meat in the destruction of the daughter of my people.”
claims.claim_text: During the siege of Jerusalem, mothers reportedly boiled and ate their own children as food, an event the text presents as fulfilling Lamentations 4:10.
claims.extractor_note: Corresponds to the account of Mary of Bethezuba in Josephus, Wars of the Jews 6.3.4.

claims.quote: Again was fulfilled the warning prophecy given fourteen centuries before: “The tender and delicate woman among you, which would not adventure to set the sole of her foot upon the ground for delicateness and tenderness, her eye shall be evil toward the husband of her bosom, and toward her son, and toward her daughter, ... and toward her children which she shall bear: for she shall eat them for want of all things secretly in the siege and straitness, wherewith thine enemy shall distress thee in thy gates.”
claims.claim_text: The text dates the Deuteronomy 28:56-57 warning about a mother eating her own children in a siege to roughly fourteen centuries before Jerusalem's AD 70 destruction, implying a composition/utterance date around the 15th-14th century BC.

claims.quote: Those prisoners who resisted when taken, were scourged, tortured, and crucified before the wall of the city. Hundreds were daily put to death in this manner,
claims.claim_text: Roman forces scourged, tortured, and crucified Jewish prisoners who resisted capture before the walls of Jerusalem, with hundreds killed daily in this manner.

claims.quote: the dreadful work continued until, along the valley of Jehoshaphat and at Calvary, crosses were erected in so great numbers that there was scarcely room to move among them.
claims.claim_text: So many crosses were erected in the valley of Jehoshaphat and at Calvary during the siege that there was scarcely room to move among them.

claims.quote: So terribly was visited that awful imprecation uttered before the judgment-seat of Pilate: “His blood be on us, and on our children.”
claims.claim_text: The text attributes to the crowd before Pilate, via Matthew 27:25, the imprecation 'His blood be on us, and on our children,' presented as fulfilled in the siege's crucifixions.

claims.quote: Titus would willingly have put an end to the fearful scene, and thus have spared Jerusalem the full measure of her doom. He was filled with horror as he saw the bodies of the dead lying in heaps in the valleys. Like one entranced, he looked from the crest of Olivet upon the magnificent temple, and gave command that not one stone of it be touched.
claims.claim_text: Titus was horrified by the piled corpses in the valleys around Jerusalem, and upon viewing the temple from the Mount of Olives, ordered that not one stone of it be touched.

claims.quote: Before attempting to gain possession of this stronghold, he made an earnest appeal to the Jewish leaders not to force him to defile the sacred place with blood. If they would come forth and fight in any other place, no Roman should violate the sanctity of the temple.
claims.claim_text: Titus appealed to the Jewish leaders to fight elsewhere so that the Romans would not have to defile the temple with bloodshed.

claims.quote: Josephus himself, in a most eloquent appeal, entreated them to surrender, to save themselves, their city, and their place of worship. But his words were answered with bitter curses. Darts were hurled at him, their last human mediator, as he stood pleading with them.
claims.claim_text: Josephus made an eloquent personal appeal urging the besieged Jews to surrender, but they responded with curses and hurled darts at him.

claims.quote: The blind obstinacy of the Jewish leaders, and the detestable crimes perpetrated within the besieged city, excited the horror and indignation of the Romans, and Titus at last decided to take the temple by storm. He determined, however, that if possible it should be saved from destruction. But his commands were disregarded.
claims.claim_text: Titus decided to take the Jerusalem temple by storm while intending to preserve it, but his preservation orders were disregarded by his soldiers.

claims.quote: After he had retired to his tent at night, the Jews, sallying from the temple, attacked the soldiers without. In the struggle, a firebrand was flung by a soldier through an opening in the porch, and immediately the cedar-lined chambers about the holy house were in a blaze.
claims.claim_text: After Titus retired for the night, Jewish fighters sallied from the temple and attacked Roman soldiers; during the fighting, a Roman soldier flung a firebrand into the temple porch, igniting the cedar-lined chambers.

claims.quote: Titus rushed to the place, followed by his generals and legionaries, and commanded the soldiers to quench the flames. His words were unheeded. In their fury the soldiers hurled blazing brands into the chambers adjoining the temple, and then with their swords they slaughtered in great numbers those who had found shelter there.
claims.claim_text: Titus rushed to the burning temple and ordered his soldiers to extinguish the flames, but they ignored him, threw more firebrands into adjoining chambers, and slaughtered large numbers of people sheltering there.

claims.quote: Blood flowed down the temple steps like water. Thousands upon thousands of Jews perished.
claims.claim_text: Blood flowed down the temple steps and thousands upon thousands of Jews died during the temple's destruction.

claims.quote: Titus found it impossible to check the rage of the soldiery; he entered with his officers, and surveyed the interior of the sacred edifice. The splendor filled them with wonder; and as the flames had not yet penetrated to the holy place, he made a last effort to save it, and springing forth, again exhorted the soldiers to stay the progress of the conflagration.
claims.claim_text: Titus, unable to control his soldiers, entered the temple with his officers, was struck by its splendor, and made a final attempt to halt the fire before it reached the holy place.

claims.quote: The centurion Liberalis endeavored to force obedience with his staff of office; but even respect for the emperor gave way to the furious animosity against the Jews, to the fierce excitement of battle, and to the insatiable hope of plunder.
claims.claim_text: The centurion Liberalis tried to force the soldiers' obedience with his staff of office, but their hostility toward the Jews, battle excitement, and desire for plunder overrode respect for Titus's authority.

claims.quote: A soldier, unperceived, thrust a lighted torch between the hinges of the door: the whole building was in flames in an instant.
claims.claim_text: An unnoticed Roman soldier thrust a lighted torch between the door hinges of the temple, setting the whole building instantly ablaze.

claims.quote: The whole summit of the hill which commanded the city, blazed like a volcano. One after another the buildings fell in, with a tremendous crash, and were swallowed up in the fiery abyss.
claims.claim_text: The temple hilltop overlooking Jerusalem blazed like a volcano as buildings collapsed one after another into the fire.

claims.quote: The number of the slain exceeded that of the slayers. The legionaries had to clamber over heaps of dead to carry on the work of extermination.
claims.claim_text: During the final destruction of the temple, the number of people killed exceeded the number of Roman soldiers doing the killing, forcing legionaries to climb over heaps of corpses to continue.

claims.quote: The leaders of the Jews forsook their impregnable towers, and Titus found them solitary. He gazed upon them with amazement, and declared that God had given them into his hands; for no engines, however powerful, could have prevailed against those stupendous battlements.
claims.claim_text: The Jewish leaders abandoned Jerusalem's supposedly impregnable towers, and Titus, finding them empty, declared that God must have delivered them into his hands since no siege engines could have taken them.

claims.quote: Both the city and the temple were razed to their foundations, and the ground upon which the holy house had stood was “plowed like a field.”
claims.claim_text: Both the city of Jerusalem and its temple were razed to their foundations, with the temple site plowed like a field.

claims.quote: In the siege and the slaughter that followed, more than a million of the people perished;
claims.claim_text: More than a million people died in the siege of Jerusalem and its aftermath.

claims.quote: the survivors were carried away as captives, sold as slaves, dragged to Rome to grace the conqueror’s triumph, thrown to wild beasts in the amphitheaters, or scattered as homeless wanderers throughout the earth.
claims.claim_text: Survivors of the fall of Jerusalem were taken captive, sold into slavery, brought to Rome for Titus's triumph, thrown to wild beasts in amphitheaters, or scattered as homeless wanderers.

claims.quote: Says the prophet, “O Israel, thou hast destroyed thyself;” “for thou hast fallen by thine iniquity.”
claims.claim_text: The text attributes to 'the prophet' (Hosea 13:9, 14:1) the statement 'O Israel, thou hast destroyed thyself... for thou hast fallen by thine iniquity.'

claims.quote: By stubborn rejection of divine love and mercy, the Jews had caused the protection of God to be withdrawn from them, and Satan was permitted to rule them according to his will.
claims.claim_text: By stubbornly rejecting divine love and mercy, the Jews caused God's protection to be withdrawn and Satan was permitted to rule them.

claims.quote: It is the restraining power of God that prevents mankind from passing fully under the control of Satan.
claims.claim_text: God's restraining power is what prevents mankind from passing fully under Satan's control.

claims.quote: The Saviour’s prophecy concerning the visitation of judgments upon Jerusalem is to have another fulfilment, of which that terrible desolation was but a faint shadow.
claims.claim_text: Christ's prophecy about judgment upon Jerusalem is to have a further, greater fulfillment beyond the AD 70 destruction, which was only a faint foreshadowing.

claims.quote: Christ has declared that He will come the second time, to gather His faithful ones to Himself: “Then shall all the tribes of the earth mourn, and they shall see the Son of man coming in the clouds of heaven with power and great glory. And He shall send His angels with a great sound of a trumpet, and they shall gather together His elect from the four winds, from one end of heaven to the other.”
claims.claim_text: The text attributes to Christ, via Matthew 24:30-31, the prophecy that at his second coming all tribes of the earth will mourn, the Son of man will appear in the clouds with power and great glory, and angels will gather his elect from the four winds.

claims.quote: Jesus declares, “There shall be signs in the sun, and in the moon, and in the stars; and upon the earth distress of nations.”
claims.claim_text: The text attributes to Jesus, via Luke 21:25, the declaration 'There shall be signs in the sun, and in the moon, and in the stars; and upon the earth distress of nations.'

claims.quote: Those who behold these harbingers of His coming are to “know that it is near, even at the doors.”
claims.claim_text: The text attributes to Jesus, via Matthew 24:33, the statement that those who see the signs of his coming should 'know that it is near, even at the doors.'

claims.quote: “Watch ye therefore,” are His words of admonition.
claims.claim_text: The text attributes to Jesus, via Mark 13:35, the admonition 'Watch ye therefore.'

claims.quote: But to them that will not watch, “the day of the Lord so cometh as a thief in the night.”
claims.claim_text: The text attributes to Paul, via 1 Thessalonians 5:2, the statement that for those who do not watch, 'the day of the Lord so cometh as a thief in the night.'

claims.quote: so shall sudden destruction come upon the careless and ungodly, “and they shall not escape.”
claims.claim_text: The text attributes to Paul, via 1 Thessalonians 5:3, the statement that sudden destruction will come upon the careless and ungodly and 'they shall not escape.'

claims.quote: The world is no more ready to credit the message for this time than were the Jews to receive the Saviour’s warning concerning Jerusalem.
claims.claim_text: The modern world is no more willing to accept the end-time message than the Jews were to accept Christ's warning about Jerusalem.
